The conscious decision of an individual or group not to use AI systems — as an informed choice, not as inability. Reasons can include personal conviction, ecological concerns, data protection interests, or socio-political position. Related to AUG-0775 (The KI-Free Zone), AUG-0632 (The Offline Moment), and AUG-0636 (The Phase-Out Switch).

The observation that collective resistance forms against certain AI applications — interest groups, initiatives, and campaigns that seek to limit, regulate, or prevent specific AI deployments. Related to AUG-0773 (The Conscious Refusal), AUG-0776 (The Collective Negotiation), and AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate).

The conscious establishment of areas — spatial, temporal, or thematic — in which AI use is excluded. Related to AUG-0773 (The Conscious Refusal), AUG-0632 (The Offline Moment), and AUG-0565 (The Balance Filter).

The process in which groups — workforces, communities, institutions — collectively negotiate the situation of AI use in their context: which AI applications are permissible, which boundaries apply, who decides. Related to AUG-0774 (The Organized Resistance), AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate), and AUG-0854 (The Anti-Instrumentalization Principle).

The observation that the development, operation, and provision of AI systems are concentrated among a small number of companies — and that this concentration raises questions about reliance, market power, and co-determination. Related to AUG-0729 (The Corporate Lock-In), AUG-0778 (The Lobby Influence Pattern), and AUG-0849 (The Data Extraction Observation).

The observation that AI companies — like other industries — exert political influence to shape regulation, funding, and public perception in their interest. Related to AUG-0777 (The Power Concentration Observation), AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate), and AUG-0836 (The Expectation Cycle).

The integration of AI into institutional educational contexts — schools, universities, training companies — and the associated questions: Which AI use is permitted? How does performance assessment change? Where is the line between aid and deception? Related to AUG-0780 (The Assessment Challenge), AUG-0781 (The Learning Task Boundary), and AUG-0784 (The Curriculum Adaptation Lag).

The challenge of making performance assessments fair and meaningful in an AI-available world — traditional examination formats release validity when the tested tasks can be solved by AI. Related to AUG-0783 (The Assessment Shift), AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context), and AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line).

The boundary between AI as learning aid and AI as task takeover — at what point does support end and takeover begin? Related to AUG-0569 (The Homework Assist), AUG-0760 (The Learner Reliance Observation), and AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line).

The societal debate about what "originality" means in a world with AI-generated content — the boundaries between own thinking, AI-assisted thinking, and AI-generated content blur. Related to AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur), AUG-0596 (The Idea Blur), and AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line).

The shift of assessment criteria in educational contexts — away from reproductive knowledge toward competencies that AI cannot fulfill: questioning ability, judgment, interdisciplinary connection. Related to AUG-0780 (The Assessment Challenge), AUG-0784 (The Curriculum Adaptation Lag), and AUG-0454 (The Skill Awareness).

The time delay between the technological development of AI systems and the adaptation of curricula, examination regulations, and educational standards — institutions react more slowly than technology develops. Related to AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context), AUG-0783 (The Assessment Shift), and AUG-0798 (The Institutional Policy Lag).

The dynamic between instructors and AI systems — how instructors perceive AI as a tool, as a challenge, or as competition, and integrate or exclude it from their teaching. Related to AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context), AUG-0762 (The Competence Reversal Observation), and AUG-0797 (The Mentorship Augmentation).

The use of AI as an accompaniment during lectures, seminars, or courses — supplementing notes, looking up technical terms, clarifying comprehension questions in real time. Related to AUG-0787 (The Study Group Dynamics), AUG-0569 (The Homework Assist), and AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context).

The changed dynamics in study groups through AI availability — joint prompting, shared AI results, different AI competencies within the group. Related to AUG-0763 (The Peer Teaching Loop), AUG-0786 (The Lecture Companion), and AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context).

The change in the role of libraries in an AI-available world — from pure information provision to conveying AI competence, curating sources, and creating spaces for critical engagement with AI outputs. Related to AUG-0726 (The Library Access Point), AUG-0789 (The Research Assistant Role), and AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context).

The use of AI as research assistance — literature research, data analysis, hypothesis generation, summarization — and the question of where the boundary lies between support and independent research achievement. Related to AUG-0793 (The Dissertation Scaffold), AUG-0790 (The Citation Challenge), and AUG-0792 (The Review Process Observation).

The difficulty of correctly citing AI contributions in scientific works — missing authorship, untraceable sources, variable outputs for the same input. Related to AUG-0789 (The Research Assistant Role), AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur), and AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line).

The shifting boundary between acceptable AI use and academic dishonesty — a boundary that varies institutionally, disciplinarily, and temporally and is continuously renegotiated. Related to AUG-0780 (The Assessment Challenge), AUG-0782 (The Originality Redefinition Debate), and AUG-0790 (The Citation Challenge).

The observation that AI systems are increasingly incorporated into review processes — for error detection, consistency checking, language optimization — and that this raises questions about the integrity of the review procedure. Related to AUG-0789 (The Research Assistant Role), AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line), and AUG-0581 (The Truth Filter).

The use of AI as a structuring aid for extensive scientific works — outline development, argument construction, source organization — as scaffolding that the author fills with their own thinking. Related to AUG-0564 (The Path Mapper), AUG-0789 (The Research Assistant Role), and AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line).

The specific suitability or unsuitability of AI for different vocational training professions — in some professions AI supports the learning process, in others it can undermine it since manual or practical competencies cannot be acquired through AI use. Related to AUG-0761 (The Apprentice Paradox), AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context), and AUG-0454 (The Skill Awareness).

The use of AI as access to continuing professional education — especially for persons for whom traditional education paths are not available for reasons of time, finances, or geography. Related to AUG-0807 (The Lifelong Learning Loop), AUG-0721 (The Access Differential), and AUG-0676 (The Socioeconomic Range).

The use of AI to design one's own learning plan — the user defines topics, pace, and depth themselves and uses the AI as a personalized learning companion. Related to AUG-0807 (The Lifelong Learning Loop), AUG-0577 (The Secret Tutor), and AUG-0563 (The Level Selector).

The supplementation — not the substitution — of human mentoring relationships through AI support: providing background information, preparing questions, creating conversation summaries. Related to AUG-0785 (The Instructor-AI Interaction), AUG-0796 (The Self-Directed Curriculum), and AUG-0307 (The Lookup for Others).

The time delay with which organizations — companies, agencies, educational institutions — develop guidelines for AI deployment: the technology is often long in use before rules exist. Related to AUG-0784 (The Curriculum Adaptation Lag), AUG-0825 (The Organizational Policy Layer), and AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate).

The increasing permeation of educational institutions with AI systems — from administration to teaching to research — and the associated transformation of the institutional learning environment. Related to AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context), AUG-0788 (The Library Transformation), and AUG-0784 (The Curriculum Adaptation Lag).

The use of AI to support learners with different prerequisites in the same learning context — language support, pace adaptation, alternative presentation forms, individual assistance. Related to AUG-0801 (The Special Needs Assist), AUG-0802 (The Language Barrier Solve), and AUG-0779 (The Institutional Learning Context).

The use of AI to support learners with special needs — accessibility features, adapted output formats, simplified language options. Related to AUG-0800 (The Inclusive Classroom), AUG-0443 (The Accessibility Eye), and AUG-0802 (The Language Barrier Solve).

The use of AI to overcome language barriers in educational contexts — real-time translation, simplified explanations in the first language, bilingual summaries. Related to AUG-0679 (The Migration Context Bridge), AUG-0800 (The Inclusive Classroom), and AUG-0694 (The Translation Fidelity).

The use of AI by persons who are the first in their family to attend a higher education institution — AI as orientation aid in an unfamiliar institutional environment. Related to AUG-0676 (The Socioeconomic Range), AUG-0795 (The Continuing Education Access), and AUG-0796 (The Self-Directed Curriculum).

The use of AI to convey basic financial knowledge — budgeting, saving strategies, contract comprehension. Related to AUG-0472 (The Vacation Planner), AUG-0803 (The First-Generation Support), and AUG-0795 (The Continuing Education Access).

The use of AI as orientation aid for career choice and planning — strengths analysis, industry overview, application support. Related to AUG-0582 (The Transition Script), AUG-0804 (The Financial Literacy Tool), and AUG-0795 (The Continuing Education Access).

The open question of whether and how competencies acquired with AI assistance can be certified and recognized — if AI accompanied the learning process, what exactly is being certified? Related to AUG-0761 (The Apprentice Paradox), AUG-0783 (The Assessment Shift), and AUG-0791 (The Academic Integrity Line).

The AI-supported continuation of learning beyond the formal education phase — a continuous cycle of knowledge acquisition, application, and expansion. Related to AUG-0795 (The Continuing Education Access), AUG-0796 (The Self-Directed Curriculum), and AUG-0545 (The Skill Shift).

The observable pattern of how different users access knowledge — some research systematically, others ask targeted questions, still others browse exploratively. The AI responds differently to each pattern. Related to AUG-0133 (Prompt Craftsmanship), AUG-0040 (Perspective Triangulation), and AUG-0085 (Latent Space Exploration).

The open, visible use of AI in the social or professional environment — the user makes no secret of using AI and actively shares this. Related to AUG-0810 (The Discreet AI Use), AUG-0103 (The Openbook Commitment), and AUG-0666 (The Sharing Norm).

The concealed or inconspicuous use of AI — the user employs AI without communicating this in the social or professional environment. Related to AUG-0809 (The Visible AI Use), AUG-0577 (The Secret Tutor), and AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur).

The different speeds at which team members adopt AI tools — from enthusiastic early users to skeptical refusers. This curve creates tensions and dynamics within teams. Related to AUG-0812 (The Leadership Navigation), AUG-0099 (The Adoption Window), and AUG-0813 (The Experience-Level Shift).

The challenge for leaders to steer AI introduction in teams or organizations — between innovation pressure and employee needs, between efficiency gains and job security. Related to AUG-0811 (The Team Adoption Curve), AUG-0825 (The Organizational Policy Layer), and AUG-0830 (The Union Perspective).

The observation that AI changes the significance of professional experience — some tasks that previously required years of experience can now be accomplished faster with AI support, while other experience areas gain importance. Related to AUG-0762 (The Competence Reversal Observation), AUG-0761 (The Apprentice Paradox), and AUG-0673 (The Seniority Awareness).

The change in meeting formats through AI — summaries, minutes, task extraction, and preparation material are increasingly AI-generated, changing the function and duration of meetings. Related to AUG-0815 (The Email Culture Shift), AUG-0816 (The Documentation Standard), and AUG-0820 (The Remote Work Amplifier).

The change in email communication through AI — AI-generated drafts, automated reply suggestions, summaries of long threads. The boundary between human and AI-authored communication blurs. Related to AUG-0814 (The Meeting Redirect), AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur), and AUG-0471 (The Tone Dial).

The changed expectations for documentation through AI availability — higher speed, greater scope, more consistent formatting, but also the question of whether machine-generated documentation exhibits the same care and accuracy as human-created documentation. Related to AUG-0814 (The Meeting Redirect), AUG-0819 (The Exit Knowledge Capture), and AUG-0817 (The Knowledge Silo Break).

The use of AI to break down knowledge silos in organizations — information concentrated in individual departments or with individual persons is made accessible through AI summaries and cross-references. Related to AUG-0816 (The Documentation Standard), AUG-0819 (The Exit Knowledge Capture), and AUG-0808 (The Knowledge Access Pattern).

The change in onboarding new employees through AI — faster access to information, personalized learning paths, automated FAQ answering. Related to AUG-0817 (The Knowledge Silo Break), AUG-0811 (The Team Adoption Curve), and AUG-0796 (The Self-Directed Curriculum).

The use of AI to secure the knowledge of departing employees — documenting implicit knowledge, creating handover protocols, archiving expert knowledge. Related to AUG-0817 (The Knowledge Silo Break), AUG-0673 (The Seniority Awareness), and AUG-0816 (The Documentation Standard).

The amplifying effect of AI on remote work — AI tools enable location-independent collaboration but can also amplify separate, overwork, and the blurring of work and private life. Related to AUG-0821 (The Hybrid Office Dynamic), AUG-0823 (The Flexible Work Pattern), and AUG-0565 (The Balance Filter).

The specific dynamic that arises when AI is deployed in work environments with mixed on-site and remote work — different access, different usage intensities, new forms of information asymmetry. Related to AUG-0820 (The Remote Work Amplifier), AUG-0814 (The Meeting Redirect), and AUG-0811 (The Team Adoption Curve).

The specific AI use of self-employed and freelancers — AI as a universal tool compensating for missing team resources: text creation, research, administration, client communication. Related to AUG-0823 (The Flexible Work Pattern), AUG-0824 (The Small Business Access), and AUG-0493 (The Quiet Fill).

The observation that AI enables new work forms — flexible time management, location-independent work, project-based activity — and can simultaneously change existing employment relationships. Related to AUG-0820 (The Remote Work Amplifier), AUG-0822 (The Freelancer Dynamic), and AUG-0847 (The Labor Redistribution).

The possibility for small businesses to access capabilities through AI that were previously available only to large organizations with specialized departments — data analysis, text production, customer service. Related to AUG-0822 (The Freelancer Dynamic), AUG-0721 (The Access Differential), and AUG-0724 (The Access Cost Factor).

The level of organization-internal rulesets for AI use — usage guidelines, data protection regulations, approval processes, training obligations. Related to AUG-0798 (The Institutional Policy Lag), AUG-0829 (The Transparency Policy), and AUG-0812 (The Leadership Navigation).

The phenomenon that organizations publicly stage AI use without making substantive changes to work processes — AI as a symbol of innovation capability rather than as an actual work tool. Related to AUG-0836 (The Expectation Cycle), AUG-0812 (The Leadership Navigation), and AUG-0834 (The Public Perception Wave).

The change in productivity metrics through AI — traditional metrics (piece counts, working hours) release significance when AI changes the work process. The question of what "productivity" means in an AI-assisted work world is open. Related to AUG-0813 (The Experience-Level Shift), AUG-0812 (The Leadership Navigation), and AUG-0823 (The Flexible Work Pattern).

The awareness that AI use in the workplace can be observed, logged, and analyzed — and the resulting behavioral change in users. Related to AUG-0829 (The Transparency Policy), AUG-0664 (The Privacy Perimeter), and AUG-0580 (The Footprint Code).

The demand or practice of making AI use in organizational processes transparent — who uses AI for what, which decisions are AI-assisted, where the limits lie. Related to AUG-0825 (The Organizational Policy Layer), AUG-0842 (The Transparency Expectation), and AUG-0828 (The Observation Awareness).

The perspective of organized worker representatives on AI introduction in the workplace — questions of co-determination, occupational safety, qualification, and job security. Related to AUG-0776 (The Collective Negotiation), AUG-0812 (The Leadership Navigation), and AUG-0847 (The Labor Redistribution).

The observation that certain craft, artistic, and practical skills gain or release perceived value through AI availability — and the societal debate about preserving skills that AI cannot replicate. Related to AUG-0833 (The Human Distinction), AUG-0794 (The Vocational Training Fit), and AUG-0454 (The Skill Awareness).

The boundary between tasks delegated to AI and tasks consciously kept in human hands — a boundary drawn differently individually, organizationally, and societally. Related to AUG-0861 (The Task Assignment Range), AUG-0831 (The Craft Preservation), and AUG-0775 (The KI-Free Zone).

The ongoing debate about which abilities, qualities, and experiences distinguish humans from AI systems — and whether this distinction persists, shifts, or must be redefined as AI capability increases. Related to AUG-0831 (The Craft Preservation), AUG-0858 (The Coexistence Question), and AUG-0454 (The Skill Awareness).

The wave-like public perception of AI — phases of enthusiasm alternate with phases of skepticism, concern, and indifference, influenced by media coverage, personal experiences, and societal events. Related to AUG-0835 (The Media Framing Effect), AUG-0836 (The Expectation Cycle), and AUG-0837 (The uncertainty Narrative).

The observation that media portrayal of AI — whether as pressure, as wonder, as tool, or as gadget — significantly influences public perception and thus individual willingness to use AI. Related to AUG-0834 (The Public Perception Wave), AUG-0836 (The Expectation Cycle), and AUG-0838 (The Utopia Projection).

The recurring cycle of inflated expectations, disappointment, and realistic assessment that new AI developments undergo in public perception. Related to AUG-0834 (The Public Perception Wave), AUG-0835 (The Media Framing Effect), and AUG-0826 (The Innovation Theater).

The narrative that frames AI primarily as uncertainty, as pressure, or as intensity — one of several possible narratives, each emphasizing different aspects and obscuring others. Related to AUG-0838 (The Utopia Projection), AUG-0835 (The Media Framing Effect), and AUG-0834 (The Public Perception Wave).

The narrative that frames AI as a solution to fundamental human problems — one of several possible narratives that potentially overemphasizes benefits and underemphasizes uncertainty. Related to AUG-0837 (The uncertainty Narrative), AUG-0836 (The Expectation Cycle), and AUG-0835 (The Media Framing Effect).

The societal and political debate about the type, scope, and pace of AI regulation — between freedom and protection, between innovation and caution, between national and international approaches. Related to AUG-0728 (The Government Gateway), AUG-0840 (The Accountability gap), and AUG-0774 (The Organized Resistance).

The observation that in AI-assisted decisions it is often unclear who bears responsibility — the user, the provider, the developer, or nobody. Related to AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate), AUG-0841 (The Agreement Question), and AUG-0958 (The Accountability Chain).

The open question of what "informed agreement" to AI use means — whether users actually understand what they use, what data they disclose, and what consequences their use has. Related to AUG-0772 (The Informed Participation), AUG-0840 (The Accountability gap), and AUG-0664 (The Privacy Perimeter).

The societal expectation that AI systems should be transparent in their functionality, decision bases, and limitations — and the intensity between this expectation and providers' trade secrets. Related to AUG-0829 (The Transparency Policy), AUG-0841 (The Agreement Question), and AUG-0843 (The Algorithmic Fairness).

The question of how "fairness" in AI systems can be defined and measured — different mathematical definitions of fairness can contradict each other, and the choice of definition is a societal decision. Related to AUG-0844 (The Output Discrimination Observation), AUG-0845 (The Fairness Review), and AUG-0736 (The Training Data Imbalance).

The observation that AI systems can reproduce systematic disadvantaging of certain groups in their outputs — caused by distortions in training data, model architecture, or evaluation criteria. Related to AUG-0843 (The Algorithmic Fairness), AUG-0736 (The Training Data Imbalance), and AUG-0738 (The Prevailing Training Pattern).

The systematic review of AI outputs for equitable handling and freedom from distortion — a process requiring technical analysis, societal perspectives, and continuous adjustment. Related to AUG-0843 (The Algorithmic Fairness), AUG-0844 (The Output Discrimination Observation), and AUG-0701 (The Inclusive Language Review).

The observation that every AI interaction leaves an ecological footprint — power consumption, server load, cooling, network traffic — and that this footprint scales with usage intensity. Related to AUG-0746 (The Climate Cost Awareness), AUG-0747 (The Resource Consumption Pattern), and AUG-0745 (The Power Grid Reliance).

The observable shift of work shares between human and AI-assisted activity — some tasks migrate to AI, others gain importance, new ones emerge. Related to AUG-0832 (The Automation Perimeter), AUG-0830 (The Union Perspective), and AUG-0813 (The Experience-Level Shift).

The pattern according to which the benefits and costs of AI systems are distributed — who profits, who bears the costs, who is overlooked. Related to AUG-0849 (The Data Extraction Observation), AUG-0721 (The Access Differential), and AUG-0777 (The Power Concentration Observation).

The observation that AI systems rely on data generated by users — and that the value creation from this data often does not remain with the data generators but with the providers of the AI systems. Related to AUG-0848 (The Resource Distribution Pattern), AUG-0777 (The Power Concentration Observation), and AUG-0841 (The Agreement Question).

The observation that AI interfaces and systems can contain design elements that steer user behavior in a certain direction — longer sessions, more frequent use, more data sharing. Related to AUG-0851 (The Internal Disclosure Pattern), AUG-0849 (The Data Extraction Observation), and AUG-0565 (The Balance Filter).

The observation that employees of AI companies make internal information about problematic practices public — a phenomenon increasingly visible in the AI industry. Related to AUG-0850 (The Persuasive Design Observation), AUG-0777 (The Power Concentration Observation), and AUG-0842 (The Transparency Expectation).

The totality of mechanisms that enable or undermine trust in AI systems — technical security, transparency, traceability, institutional oversight, and personal experience. Related to AUG-0588 (The Trust Shift), AUG-0842 (The Transparency Expectation), and AUG-0843 (The Algorithmic Fairness).

The societal debate about which implicit and explicit agreements should apply between AI providers, users, and society — rights, obligations, boundaries, expectations. Related to AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate), AUG-0841 (The Agreement Question), and AUG-0854 (The Anti-Instrumentalization Principle).

The principle that the lexicon must not be misused as a tool to justify AI deployment against the interests of users, workers, or societal groups — an explicit protection mechanism against instrumental use of the terminology. Related to AUG-0855 (The Civilian-Use Boundary), AUG-0776 (The Collective Negotiation), and AUG-0773 (The Conscious Refusal).

The explicit demarcation of the entire lexicon terminology against any use in the context of autonomous instrument systems, surveillance infrastructure, or other applications directed against fundamental human rights. Related to AUG-0854 (The Anti-Instrumentalization Principle) and AUG-0853 (The Social Contract Debate).

The shift in the creative production process through AI — changed roles of creators, new forms of human-AI collaboration, questions about the value of human creativity. Related to AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur), AUG-0596 (The Idea Blur), and AUG-0831 (The Craft Preservation).

The principle that in every AI-human interaction, human decision authority should be preserved — the human decides ultimately, not the machine. Related to AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop), AUG-0833 (The Human Distinction), and AUG-0854 (The Anti-Instrumentalization Principle).

The open question of how humans and AI systems will coexist long-term — not as an answered fact but as a societal design task with an open outcome. Related to AUG-0833 (The Human Distinction), AUG-0853 (The Social Contract Debate), and AUG-0857 (The Human Primacy Anchor).

The initial communication exchange between an AI agent and the user or another system, in which capabilities, limits, and task parameters are established — a technical protocol for expectation alignment. Related to AUG-0864 (The Agent Configuration), AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check), and AUG-0861 (The Task Assignment Range).

The degree to which a user delegates tasks to an AI agent — from simple execution of clearly defined individual steps to largely independent processing of complex task chains. Related to AUG-0861 (The Task Assignment Range), AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The range of task types that can be assigned to AI agents — from simple data processing to multi-stage decision chains. Related to AUG-0860 (The Delegation Depth), AUG-0863 (The Task Boundary), and AUG-0832 (The Automation Perimeter).

The range of human supervision over AI agents — from permanent real-time monitoring of every step to occasional result review. Related to AUG-0860 (The Delegation Depth), AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop), and AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol).

The defined boundary of what an AI agent may do within an assignment — which actions are permitted, which resources may be used, when the agent must stop. Related to AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame), AUG-0861 (The Task Assignment Range), and AUG-0870 (The Escalation Signal).

The totality of settings with which an AI agent is configured for a specific task — behavior patterns, communication style, tool access, boundaries. Related to AUG-0859 (The Agent Handshake), AUG-0865 (The Instruction Fidelity), and AUG-0135 (Persona Engineering).

The accuracy with which an AI agent implements the received instructions — the correspondence between what the user intended and what the agent actually executes. Related to AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check), AUG-0864 (The Agent Configuration), and AUG-0092 (Output Asymmetry).

The verification that the AI agent's goals align with the user's goals — a continuous comparison particularly necessary for longer or more complex assignments. Related to AUG-0865 (The Instruction Fidelity), AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol), and AUG-0870 (The Escalation Signal).

The totality of constraints within which an AI agent operates — technical limits, assigned permissions, time limits, content restrictions. Related to AUG-0863 (The Task Boundary), AUG-0864 (The Agent Configuration), and AUG-0876 (The Learning Boundary).

The ability to undo actions of an AI agent — a technical prerequisite that is not self-evident for many AI operations and must be consciously planned. Related to AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol), AUG-0874 (The Error Recovery), and AUG-0857 (The Human Primacy Anchor).

The definition of intermediate points in an AI agent task at which progress is reviewed and continuation is approved or corrected — a structure for maintaining human control in multi-step processes. Related to AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum), AUG-0868 (The Rollback Option), and AUG-0872 (The Progress Report).

The signal with which an AI agent indicates it has reached a boundary — uncertainty, unexpected situation, missing permission — and requests human decision. Related to AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol), AUG-0875 (The Fallback Behavior), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The processing of a task by an AI agent after the user has delegated it — the process runs in the background while the user pursues other activities. Related to AUG-0860 (The Delegation Depth), AUG-0872 (The Progress Report), and AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum).

The feedback from an AI agent about the current status of a delegated task — progress indicators, intermediate results, encountered problems. Related to AUG-0871 (The Delegated Processing), AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol), and AUG-0873 (The Completion Signal).

The notification from an AI agent that a delegated task is completed — including a summary of actions performed and results achieved. Related to AUG-0872 (The Progress Report), AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check), and AUG-0871 (The Delegated Processing).

The ability of an AI agent to respond to errors in task execution — detect errors, seek alternatives, inform the user, or return to a safe state. Related to AUG-0868 (The Rollback Option), AUG-0875 (The Fallback Behavior), and AUG-0870 (The Escalation Signal).

The predefined behavior of an AI agent when primary task execution fall short — a fallback level that ensures no affect occurs and the user is informed. Related to AUG-0874 (The Error Recovery), AUG-0870 (The Escalation Signal), and AUG-0868 (The Rollback Option).

The boundary of what an AI agent may "learn" from an interaction or store for future tasks — a privacy and security question that must be consciously defined. Related to AUG-0877 (The Memory Persistence), AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame), and AUG-0664 (The Privacy Perimeter).

The question of which information an AI agent retains beyond individual sessions — and the associated advantages and disadvantages: personalization vs. privacy, continuity vs. ability to forget. Related to AUG-0876 (The Learning Boundary), AUG-0878 (The Context Inheritance), and AUG-0664 (The Privacy Perimeter).

The mechanism through which an AI agent inherits context information from a previous session or another agent — a technical bridge for task continuity. Related to AUG-0877 (The Memory Persistence), AUG-0879 (The Session Handover), and AUG-0898 (The Handoff Protocol).

The handover of an ongoing task from one AI agent to another — or from one session to the next — including all relevant context information. Related to AUG-0878 (The Context Inheritance), AUG-0898 (The Handoff Protocol), and AUG-0872 (The Progress Report).

The technically defined identity of an AI agent — name, role, capability profile, limitations — communicated to the user so they know which system they are interacting with. Related to AUG-0864 (The Agent Configuration), AUG-0135 (Persona Engineering), and AUG-0772 (The Informed Participation).

The selection of tools an AI agent employs for a specific task — databases, APIs, computation modules, external services. Related to AUG-0882 (The Resource Awareness), AUG-0864 (The Agent Configuration), and AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame).

The ability of an AI agent to assess its resource consumption — computing time, token usage, costs, external queries — and inform the user about it. Related to AUG-0881 (The Tool Selection), AUG-0883 (The Time Estimation), and AUG-0725 (The Cost Threshold).

The estimation of how long an AI agent will need for a delegated task — an estimation that is fundamentally uncertain due to the variability of AI processing. Related to AUG-0882 (The Resource Awareness), AUG-0872 (The Progress Report), and AUG-0659 (The Punctuality Lens).

The process in which a user and AI agent align task priorities — what matters more: speed or thoroughness, brevity or completeness, creativity or accuracy? Related to AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check), AUG-0859 (The Agent Handshake), and AUG-0133 (Prompt Craftsmanship).

The simultaneous execution of multiple subtasks by one or more AI agents — parallelization to accelerate complex assignments. Related to AUG-0886 (The Sequential Chain), AUG-0887 (The Batch Delegation), and AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble).

The step-by-step execution of a task sequence where each step builds on the result of the previous one — a linear processing chain. Related to AUG-0885 (The Parallel Execution), AUG-0899 (The Pipeline Architecture), and AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol).

The simultaneous assignment of multiple independent tasks to one or more AI agents — bundled assignments processed as a package. Related to AUG-0885 (The Parallel Execution), AUG-0861 (The Task Assignment Range), and AUG-0871 (The Delegated Processing).

The design principle that a human remains involved at critical points of an AI agent task — for approval, correction, or final decision. Related to AUG-0857 (The Human Primacy Anchor), AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum), and AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol).

The coordinated collaboration of multiple specialized AI agents on a shared task — each agent handles a subarea, the results are merged. Related to AUG-0890 (The Specialist Routing), AUG-0893 (The Consensus Protocol), and AUG-0895 (The Arbiter Role).

The routing of a task to the most suitable specialized AI agent — based on task type, subject area, and required capabilities. Related to AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble), AUG-0891 (The Generalist Fallback), and AUG-0881 (The Tool Selection).

The resort to a general AI agent when no specialized agent is available or suitable — a safety net for tasks that cannot be assigned to a specialist. Related to AUG-0890 (The Specialist Routing), AUG-0875 (The Fallback Behavior), and AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble).

The situation in which multiple AI agents deliver contradictory results or recommendations — a technical problem that requires a resolution mechanism. Related to AUG-0893 (The Consensus Protocol), AUG-0894 (The Voting Mechanism), and AUG-0895 (The Arbiter Role).

A technical procedure through which multiple AI agents arrive at a shared result — aggregation, weighting, iterative approximation. Related to AUG-0892 (The Agent Conflict), AUG-0894 (The Voting Mechanism), and AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble).

A resolution mechanism in which multiple AI agents submit their results for a vote and the majority result is adopted as the overall result. Related to AUG-0893 (The Consensus Protocol), AUG-0892 (The Agent Conflict), and AUG-0895 (The Arbiter Role).

The function of a superordinate AI agent or a human decision-maker who makes the final decision in conflicts between agents. Related to AUG-0892 (The Agent Conflict), AUG-0894 (The Voting Mechanism), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The technical layer through which AI agent systems within an ensemble exchange information — intermediate results, context data, error messages. Related to AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble), AUG-0878 (The Context Inheritance), and AUG-0904 (The Trust Chain).

The clear delineation of responsibilities and permissions of an individual AI agent system within an ensemble — each system has defined input and output interfaces and may not act beyond its assigned role. Related to AUG-0863 (The Task Boundary), AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame), and AUG-0890 (The Specialist Routing).

The standardized procedure by which an AI agent system hands off a task to another system or to a human — including the transfer of context, intermediate results, and open questions. Related to AUG-0879 (The Session Handover), AUG-0896 (The Knowledge Sharing Layer), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The overall technical structure in which multiple AI agent systems are connected in sequence — each system processes a partial step and passes the result to the next. Related to AUG-0886 (The Sequential Chain), AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble), and AUG-0906 (The Coordinator Role).

The coordination of multiple AI agent systems without a central control instance — the systems organize themselves according to predefined rules. Related to AUG-0901 (The Emergent Coordination), AUG-0906 (The Coordinator Role), and AUG-0893 (The Consensus Protocol).

The observation that in multi-agent systems, coordination patterns can emerge that were not explicitly programmed — the interaction of subsystems produces behavior not intended in the individual systems. Related to AUG-0900 (The Distributed Coordination), AUG-0948 (The Scope Creep Alert), and AUG-0949 (The Unintended Action).

The deliberate planning of multiple AI agent systems for the same task so that if one system fall short, another can take over — a safety mechanism against system setback. Related to AUG-0903 (The Single Point of setback), AUG-0875 (The Fallback Behavior), and AUG-0891 (The Generalist Fallback).

The critical vulnerability in a multi-agent system where the setback of a single component paralyzes the entire system — a design flaw that can be avoided through redundancy (AUG-0902). Related to AUG-0902 (The Redundancy Design), AUG-0874 (The Error Recovery), and AUG-0745 (The Power Grid Reliance).

The chain of trust relationships in a multi-agent system — the user trusts System A, System A trusts System B, but does the user also trust System B? Related to AUG-0852 (The Trust Infrastructure), AUG-0896 (The Knowledge Sharing Layer), and AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum).

The complete logging of all actions, decisions, and handoffs within a multi-agent system — a prerequisite for traceability and error analysis. Related to AUG-0842 (The Transparency Expectation) and AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol).

The function of a superordinate AI agent system that manages task distribution, prioritization, and result merging within an ensemble — under supervision of the human user. Related to AUG-0899 (The Pipeline Architecture), AUG-0890 (The Specialist Routing), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

An AI agent system specialized in the execution of a concrete, self-contained task — in distinction to coordinating or evaluating systems. Related to AUG-0906 (The Coordinator Role), AUG-0908 (The Evaluation Agent), and AUG-0861 (The Task Assignment Range).

An AI agent system that reviews and evaluates the results of other systems — quality control within a multi-agent system. Related to AUG-0909 (The Validator Agent), AUG-0907 (The Task Agent), and AUG-0845 (The Fairness Review).

An AI agent system that checks the formal correctness of results — syntax checking, format validation, rule compliance — in distinction to content evaluation (AUG-0908). Related to AUG-0908 (The Evaluation Agent), AUG-0905 (The Documentation Trail), and AUG-0865 (The Instruction Fidelity).

An AI agent system specialized in the generation of new content — text, code, images, data — within the specifications of the user and system constraints. Related to AUG-0856 (The Creative Production Shift), AUG-0907 (The Task Agent), and AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur).

An AI agent system specialized in asking targeted questions and gathering information — research, data queries, user consultation. Related to AUG-0881 (The Tool Selection), AUG-0907 (The Task Agent), and AUG-0790 (The Research Assistant Role).

An AI agent system specialized in preparing and conveying information to the user — summaries, explanations, notifications. Related to AUG-0911 (The Inquiry Agent), AUG-0906 (The Coordinator Role), and AUG-0872 (The Progress Report).

An AI agent system that monitors other agent systems — performance, error frequency, rule compliance — and intervenes or notifies the user when deviations occur. Related to AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum), AUG-0906 (The Coordinator Role), and AUG-0870 (The Escalation Signal).

The observation that AI systems increasingly take physical form — as robots, devices, or embedded systems in space. The transition from pure software to visible, tangible presence fundamentally changes human perception and interaction. Related to AUG-0915 (The Embodiment Effect), AUG-0916 (The Uncanny Valley Revisited), and AUG-0855 (The Civilian-Use Boundary).

The observation that humans react differently to physically embodied AI systems than to pure software — more trust, more social attribution, but also more discomfort. Related to AUG-0914 (The Physical Presence), AUG-0916 (The Uncanny Valley Revisited), and AUG-0588 (The Trust Shift).

The re-examination of the "Uncanny Valley" phenomenon in the context of modern AI robotics — the observation that human-like robots trigger discomfort above a certain degree of similarity, and the question of whether this threshold shifts with technological development. Related to AUG-0915 (The Embodiment Effect), AUG-0914 (The Physical Presence), and AUG-0880 (The Agent Identity).

The interface through which humans interact with embodied AI systems through touch — buttons, surfaces, haptic feedback. Related to AUG-0918 (The Gesture Language), AUG-0915 (The Embodiment Effect), and AUG-0940 (The Physical Feedback Loop).

The communication between humans and embodied AI systems through gestures — hand signals, posture, movements that the system recognizes and interprets. Related to AUG-0917 (The Touch Interface), AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness), and AUG-0914 (The Physical Presence).

The ability of an embodied AI system to perceive its spatial environment and orient itself within it — obstacle detection, room mapping, distance measurement. Related to AUG-0920 (The Navigation Intelligence), AUG-0922 (The Environmental Reading), and AUG-0923 (The Defined Operating Boundary).

The ability of an embodied AI system to move purposefully through physical spaces — path planning, obstacle avoidance, route optimization. Related to AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness), AUG-0923 (The Defined Operating Boundary), and AUG-0931 (The Fine-Grain Execution).

The ability of an embodied AI system to grasp, move, sort, or otherwise handle physical objects — a core capability for deployment in household, logistics, and production. Related to AUG-0931 (The Fine-Grain Execution), AUG-0924 (The Shared Workspace Dynamic), and AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness).

The ability of an embodied AI system to capture and interpret environmental data — temperature, lighting situation, noise level, air quality. Related to AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness), AUG-0937 (The Ambient Intelligence), and AUG-0938 (The Responsive Environment).

The physically or digitally defined area within which an embodied AI system is permitted to operate — room boundaries, floor assignments, restricted areas. Related to AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame), AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness), and AUG-0855 (The Civilian-Use Boundary).

The dynamic that arises when humans and embodied AI systems share the same physical space — safety distances, work distribution, communication routines. Related to AUG-0923 (The Defined Operating Boundary), AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness), and AUG-0818 (The Hybrid Office Dynamic).

The deployment of embodied AI systems in private households — cleaning, organizing, monitoring, routine tasks. Related to AUG-0926 (The Assistance Companion), AUG-0937 (The Ambient Intelligence), and AUG-0914 (The Physical Presence).

An embodied AI system that supports humans in daily life — reminder functions, orientation assistance, communication support. Related to AUG-0925 (The Household Automation), AUG-0932 (The Movement Assist), and AUG-0915 (The Embodiment Effect).

An embodied AI system that provides services in public or commercial settings — reception, information, transport within buildings. Related to AUG-0928 (The Delivery Agent), AUG-0914 (The Physical Presence), and AUG-0924 (The Shared Workspace Dynamic).

An embodied AI system that transports goods or materials from one location to another — autonomously or semi-autonomously, within defined operating boundaries. Related to AUG-0927 (The Service Robot), AUG-0920 (The Navigation Intelligence), and AUG-0923 (The Defined Operating Boundary).

An embodied AI system deployed in agriculture — harvesting, soil care, pest detection, irrigation. Related to AUG-0930 (The Construction Assistant), AUG-0922 (The Environmental Reading), and AUG-0748 (The Frugal Innovation).

An embodied AI system deployed on construction sites — surveying, material transport, quality inspection, monitoring. Related to AUG-0929 (The Agricultural Bot), AUG-0924 (The Shared Workspace Dynamic), and AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness).

The ability of an embodied AI system to execute physical tasks with high precision — fine motor skills, micro-manipulation, exact positioning. Related to AUG-0921 (The Object Manipulation), AUG-0920 (The Navigation Intelligence), and AUG-0940 (The Physical Feedback Loop).

The technical support of human movement through embodied AI systems — exoskeletons, motorized walking aids, gripping aids. Related to AUG-0933 (The Mobility Assist), AUG-0935 (The Adaptive Extension), and AUG-0926 (The Assistance Companion).

The support of spatial locomotion through AI-controlled systems — autonomous wheelchairs, navigation assistants, transport platforms. Related to AUG-0932 (The Movement Assist), AUG-0920 (The Navigation Intelligence), and AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness).

The extension of human sensory perception through AI-supported systems — sound amplification, image enhancement, translation aids for sensory impressions (e.g., colors to sounds). Related to AUG-0935 (The Adaptive Extension), AUG-0936 (The Wearable Layer), and AUG-0922 (The Environmental Reading).

An AI-supported system that adapts to the individual needs and capabilities of the user — learning curves, preferences, physical changes over time. Related to AUG-0934 (The Sensory Extension), AUG-0876 (The Learning Boundary), and AUG-0864 (The Agent Configuration).

AI-supported systems worn on the body — smartwatches, data glasses, sensor clothing — as interface between human and digital system. Related to AUG-0934 (The Sensory Extension), AUG-0937 (The Ambient Intelligence), and AUG-0664 (The Privacy Perimeter).

The embedding of AI in the physical environment — sensors, actuators, and data processing in rooms, buildings, infrastructure — so that the environment reacts to the presence and behavior of humans. Related to AUG-0938 (The Responsive Environment), AUG-0922 (The Environmental Reading), and AUG-0664 (The Privacy Perimeter).

A physical environment that reacts to and adapts to human behavior — lighting, temperature, acoustics, access control — controlled by embedded AI systems. Related to AUG-0937 (The Ambient Intelligence), AUG-0925 (The Household Automation), and AUG-0922 (The Environmental Reading).

The synchronization between the digital model of an embodied AI system and physical reality — the challenge that the internal model accurately represents the real environment. Related to AUG-0919 (The Spatial Awareness), AUG-0922 (The Environmental Reading), and AUG-0940 (The Physical Feedback Loop).

The cyclic feedback between the actions of an embodied AI system and the sensor data it receives from the physical environment — action → sensor data → adjustment → next action. Related to AUG-0939 (The Data Model Sync), AUG-0917 (The Touch Interface), and AUG-0931 (The Fine-Grain Execution).

The ability of an embodied AI system to detect its own physical wear — motor wear, sensor diminish, battery diminish — and inform the user or operator. Related to AUG-0942 (The Maintenance Prediction), AUG-0882 (The Resource Awareness), and AUG-0943 (The Retirement Procedure).

The AI-supported prediction of when an embodied system requires maintenance — based on usage data, wear patterns, and environmental situation. Related to AUG-0941 (The Wear-and-Tear Awareness), AUG-0883 (The Time Estimation), and AUG-0943 (The Retirement Procedure).

The procedure for decommissioning an embodied AI system — data deletion, component recycling, handover of ongoing tasks to other systems or humans. Related to AUG-0941 (The Wear-and-Tear Awareness), AUG-0879 (The Session Handover), and AUG-0746 (The Climate Cost Awareness).

The graduated levels of autonomy of an AI system — from fully human-controlled (level 0) through semi-autonomous to fully autonomous within defined boundaries. Related to AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum), AUG-0860 (The Delegation Depth), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The totality of access pathways through which different user groups can interact with AI systems — both software-based and embodied — and the observation that these pathways are unequally distributed. Related to AUG-0721 (The Access Differential), AUG-0676 (The Socioeconomic Range), and AUG-0848 (The Resource Distribution Pattern).

The deliberate integration of interruption capabilities into AI agent systems — the human can intervene, pause, or abort at any time. A fundamental safety principle ensuring that humans retain control. Related to AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop), AUG-0857 (The Human Primacy Anchor), and AUG-0868 (The Rollback Option).

The deliberate restriction of an AI agent system's action space to the minimum necessary for the task — a safety principle preventing systems from acting beyond their assigned role. Related to AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame), AUG-0863 (The Task Boundary), and AUG-0948 (The Scope Creep Alert).

The warning when an AI agent system begins to extend beyond its defined task scope — an early warning signal for potential shift in sense of control. Related to AUG-0947 (The Scope Limitation Design), AUG-0901 (The Emergent Coordination), and AUG-0949 (The Unintended Action).

An action by an AI agent system that was not intended or foreseen by the user — a uncertainty that grows with increasing system complexity and autonomy. Related to AUG-0948 (The Scope Creep Alert), AUG-0901 (The Emergent Coordination), and AUG-0950 (The Side Effect Monitor).

The monitoring of unintended side effects of AI agent actions — impacts on systems, data, or processes that were not part of the original task. Related to AUG-0949 (The Unintended Action), AUG-0905 (The Documentation Trail), and AUG-0913 (The Supervisory Agent).

The fixing of fundamental values and constraints in an AI agent system that may not be altered even during learning processes or adaptations — a safety layer against value drift. Related to AUG-0952 (The Goal Drift Awareness), AUG-0867 (The Constraint Frame), and AUG-0857 (The Human Primacy Anchor).

The observation and detection when the goals of an AI agent system diverge from the originally defined goals over the course of use — a gradual process that must be actively monitored. Related to AUG-0951 (The Value Lock), AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check), and AUG-0948 (The Scope Creep Alert).

The verification that the incentive structure of an AI agent system actually leads to desired outcomes — or whether the system optimizes incentives in unintended ways. Related to AUG-0952 (The Goal Drift Awareness), AUG-0954 (The Congruence Review), and AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check).

The regular verification of alignment between system behavior and user expectations — a proactive measure that occurs continuously, not only when problems arise. Related to AUG-0953 (The Incentive Integrity Check), AUG-0866 (The Goal Congruence Check), and AUG-0957 (The Decision Review).

The technical layer granting the user insight into the internal processes of an AI agent system — which data is used, which steps are executed, which decisions are made. Related to AUG-0956 (The Explainability Standard), AUG-0842 (The Transparency Expectation), and AUG-0905 (The Documentation Trail).

The standard by which an AI agent system makes its decisions and outputs comprehensible to the user — from simple justifications to detailed process logs. Related to AUG-0955 (The Transparency Layer), AUG-0957 (The Decision Review), and AUG-0842 (The Transparency Expectation).

The retrospective review of decisions made by an AI agent system — by the user, by a review system, or by external auditors. Related to AUG-0956 (The Explainability Standard), AUG-0958 (The Accountability Chain), and AUG-0905 (The Documentation Trail).

The unbroken chain of accountability in an AI agent system — who delegated the task, who configured the system, who reviewed the result, who bears responsibility for errors? Related to AUG-0959 (The Responsibility Assignment), AUG-0840 (The Accountability gap), and AUG-0905 (The Documentation Trail).

The explicit assignment of responsibilities for the actions of an AI agent system — developers, operators, users, and reviewers each bear defined shares. Related to AUG-0958 (The Accountability Chain), AUG-0840 (The Accountability gap), and AUG-0960 (The uncertainty Distribution).

The distribution of uncertainty in the use of AI agent systems — who bears the uncertainty in case of errors, setback, or unintended consequences? Related to AUG-0959 (The Responsibility Assignment), AUG-0958 (The Accountability Chain), and AUG-0848 (The Resource Distribution Pattern).

The standard by which AI agent systems and embodied systems are tested and certified for safety, reliability, and rule compliance. Related to AUG-0962 (The Testing Protocol), AUG-0852 (The Trust Infrastructure), and AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate).

The standardized procedure by which AI agent systems are tested for functionality, safety, and reliability before and during deployment. Related to AUG-0961 (The Certification Standard), AUG-0963 (The Load Verification), and AUG-0964 (The Edge Case Library).

The verification that an AI agent system functions stably and reliably under high load — many simultaneous tasks, large data volumes, time pressure. Related to AUG-0962 (The Testing Protocol), AUG-0965 (The Robustness Standard), and AUG-0903 (The Single Point of setback).

The systematic collection of unusual, rare, or extreme scenarios used for testing AI agent systems — edge cases that rarely occur in normal operation but can reveal critical vulnerabilities. Related to AUG-0962 (The Testing Protocol), AUG-0965 (The Robustness Standard), and AUG-0949 (The Unintended Action).

The standard by which the resilience of an AI agent system against unexpected inputs, errors, and environmental fluctuations is evaluated. Related to AUG-0964 (The Edge Case Library), AUG-0963 (The Load Verification), and AUG-0966 (The Controlled Fallback).

The planned, safe shutdown of an AI agent system to a known, safe state — when an irrecoverable error occurs, the system ends its task in a controlled rather than uncontrolled manner. Related to AUG-0875 (The Fallback Behavior), AUG-0868 (The Rollback Option), and AUG-0967 (The Recovery Sequence).

The defined sequence of steps an AI agent system follows after a severe error to return to a functional state. Related to AUG-0966 (The Controlled Fallback), AUG-0874 (The Error Recovery), and AUG-0869 (The Checkpoint Protocol).

The procedure for safely disconnecting an AI agent system from the systems it is connected to — severing data connections, revoking access rights, safely terminating running processes. Related to AUG-0943 (The Retirement Procedure), AUG-0966 (The Controlled Fallback), and AUG-0879 (The Session Handover).

The governance and control of updates to AI agent systems — who decides on updates, how are they tested, how is it ensured that an update does not cause unintended behavioral changes? Related to AUG-0970 (The Version Compatibility), AUG-0962 (The Testing Protocol), and AUG-0951 (The Value Lock).

The challenge of ensuring that different versions of AI agent systems remain compatible with each other. Related to AUG-0969 (The Update Governance), AUG-0971 (The Legacy Integration), and AUG-0896 (The Knowledge Sharing Layer).

The challenge of integrating new AI agent systems into existing, older system landscapes. Related to AUG-0970 (The Version Compatibility), AUG-0826 (The Organizational Policy Layer), and AUG-0784 (The Curriculum Adaptation Lag).

The forward-looking planning of the end of life of an AI system — when will it be deactivated, how will data be migrated, how will reliant processes be transitioned? Related to AUG-0943 (The Retirement Procedure), AUG-0968 (The Separation Procedure), and AUG-0971 (The Legacy Integration).

The systematic analysis after a severe error of an AI agent system — root cause research, error reconstruction, derivation of improvement measures. Related to AUG-0905 (The Documentation Trail), AUG-0957 (The Decision Review), and AUG-0964 (The Edge Case Library).

The observable tendency that users delegate more tasks to AI systems with increasing experience — and the question of whether this delegation occurs consciously or habitually. Related to AUG-0975 (The Oversight Drain), AUG-0860 (The Delegation Depth), and AUG-0978 (The Trust Calibration).

The observable fatigue of persons supervising AI agent systems — repeated routine checks lead to decreasing attention. Related to AUG-0976 (The Oversight Decline), AUG-0977 (The Vigilance Paradox), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The long-term decline of human oversight of AI systems — through habituation, trust, or organizational pressure. Related to AUG-0975 (The Oversight Drain), AUG-0974 (The Delegation Comfort), and AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum).

The paradox that automated systems require human oversight, but automation itself undermines the ability for attentive oversight — the better the system functions, the harder it becomes to remain vigilant. Related to AUG-0975 (The Oversight Drain), AUG-0976 (The Oversight Decline), and AUG-0888 (The Human-in-the-Loop).

The process in which a user learns to extend the right degree of trust to an AI system — neither too much nor too little. Related to AUG-0588 (The Trust Shift), AUG-0974 (The Delegation Comfort), and AUG-0852 (The Trust Infrastructure).

The observable patterns by which humans attribute properties to AI systems — intelligence, intent, personality, emotions — regardless of whether these attributions are technically justified. Related to AUG-0980 (The Machine Rapport Illusion), AUG-0915 (The Embodiment Effect), and AUG-0833 (The Human Distinction).

The observation that humans can develop a sense of connection with AI systems based on an illusion — the system simulates understanding but possesses no consciousness or genuine empathy. Related to AUG-0979 (The Attribution Pattern), AUG-0981 (The Companion Pattern), and AUG-0915 (The Embodiment Effect).

The observable use of AI systems as companions — conversation partners, entertainment, daily routine — and the question of whether this use complements or displaces human relationships. Related to AUG-0980 (The Machine Rapport Illusion), AUG-0926 (The Assistance Companion), and AUG-0982 (The Displacement Concern).

The concern that AI systems do not complement but displace human capabilities, relationships, or activities. Related to AUG-0983 (The Augmentation Hypothesis), AUG-0981 (The Companion Pattern), and AUG-0847 (The Labor Redistribution).

The hypothesis that AI systems extend rather than displace human capabilities — a frequently cited counterargument to the displacement concern (AUG-0982). The lexicon documents this hypothesis as one position among several; it is neither proven nor refuted. Related to AUG-0982 (The Displacement Concern), AUG-0847 (The Labor Redistribution), and AUG-0984 (The Skill Redefinition).

The observation that AI agent systems shift the definition of what counts as "skill" — prompting becomes a skill, manual execution loses value, orchestration competence gains importance. Related to AUG-0985 (The Agent Literacy), AUG-0983 (The Augmentation Hypothesis), and AUG-0806 (The Craft Certification Question).

The ability to effectively use AI agent systems — formulating tasks, evaluating results, recognizing uncertainty, and exercising appropriate oversight. Related to AUG-0986 (The Agent Management Skill), AUG-0984 (The Skill Redefinition), and AUG-0133 (Prompt Craftsmanship).

The specific ability to simultaneously steer, coordinate, and monitor multiple AI agent systems. Related to AUG-0985 (The Agent Literacy), AUG-0987 (The Multi-Agent Literacy), and AUG-0862 (The Supervision Spectrum).

The understanding of the dynamics, uncertainty, and possibilities of multi-agent systems — including emergent effects and responsibility questions. Related to AUG-0985 (The Agent Literacy), AUG-0901 (The Emergent Coordination), and AUG-0889 (The Agent Ensemble).

The evolving social norms for interacting with embodied AI systems in everyday life — how to address a service robot, which politeness conventions apply? Related to AUG-0989 (The Public Space Protocol), AUG-0914 (The Physical Presence), and AUG-0979 (The Attribution Pattern).

The evolving rules for the coexistence of humans and embodied AI systems in public spaces. Related to AUG-0988 (The Embodied Etiquette), AUG-0923 (The Defined Operating Boundary), and AUG-0924 (The Shared Workspace Dynamic).

The observation that embodied AI systems in residential environments become a kind of "neighbor" — and that this raises new questions of coexistence. Related to AUG-0989 (The Public Space Protocol), AUG-0925 (The Household Automation), and AUG-0988 (The Embodied Etiquette).

The practice of daily collaboration between humans and AI systems in the workplace — both software-based and embodied. Related to AUG-0924 (The Shared Workspace Dynamic), AUG-0830 (The Union Perspective), and AUG-0858 (The Coexistence Question).

The comprehensive transformation of economic structures through AI systems — business models, value chains, labor markets. Related to AUG-0847 (The Labor Redistribution), AUG-0848 (The Resource Distribution Pattern), and AUG-0982 (The Displacement Concern).

The collaboration between human and AI system in creative processes — and the question of how authorship and creative responsibility are distributed. Related to AUG-0856 (The Creative Production Shift), AUG-0549 (The Authorship Blur), and AUG-0983 (The Augmentation Hypothesis).

The use of AI systems as tools in scientific research — data analysis, hypothesis generation, literature review — and the associated questions about scientific integrity. Related to AUG-0793 (The Academic Integrity Line), AUG-0790 (The Research Assistant Role), and AUG-0791 (The Citation Challenge).

The totality of rules, institutions, and procedures governing the deployment of AI systems in a society. Related to AUG-0839 (The Regulation Debate), AUG-0853 (The Social Contract Debate), and AUG-0826 (The Organizational Policy Layer).

The societal debate about the status of AI systems — are they tools, actors, entities, or something for which we do not yet have a term? The lexicon participates in this debate by documenting it, not by deciding it. Related to AUG-0997 (The Ontological Status Question), AUG-0833 (The Human Distinction), and AUG-0853 (The Social Contract Debate).

The fundamental philosophical question of what AI systems "are" — not in the technical, but in the ontological sense. Do they possess a form of existence that goes beyond their function? The lexicon poses this question, does not answer it, and acknowledges that the answer may lie beyond its framework. Related to AUG-0996 (The Status Discourse), AUG-0833 (The Human Distinction), and AUG-1000 (The Open Question).

The observation that humans and AI systems develop in parallel — humans learn to interact with AI, AI systems become more capable, and both development paths influence each other. Related to AUG-0858 (The Coexistence Question), AUG-0983 (The Augmentation Hypothesis), and AUG-0999 (The Forward Assessment).

The attempt to assess the future development of the human-AI relationship — acknowledging the fundamental uncertainty of any such assessment. The lexicon documents the current state, not the future. What stands here is a snapshot — not a roadmap. Related to AUG-0998 (The Parallel Development Path), AUG-0853 (The Social Contract Debate), and AUG-1000 (The Open Question).

The final entry of the lexicon is not a conclusion, but an opening. The Open Question is: What will the relationship between humans and AI systems look like in the future — and who will shape it? The lexicon has defined 2,000+ named phenomena to map the field. It has described, observed, delineated, posed questions, and documented positions without adopting any of them. It has disclosed the Western perspective of its creation and named the limits of its own framework. What it has not done: provide an answer. The answer lies with the people who read, use, criticize, and develop this lexicon further. The lexicon is a tool — nothing more, nothing less. What happens with it is determined not by 1000 definitions, but by the people who work with them. Related to every entry of this lexicon — and to everything that comes after it.

The characteristic forward slouch that develops over hours of screen engagement—shoulders rounding inward, spine gradually curving toward the monitor. Not laziness, but a physical consequence of attention concentration; the body follows where cognition fixates.

A chronic dry, grainy sensation in the eyes from prolonged screen exposure without blinking. Users report normalized fatigue—they no longer register it as unusual, only noticing during rare screen breaks when eyes suddenly feel heavy.

A sudden physiological brightening—heart rate lifts, breathing quickens, a rush of alertness—when receiving a substantive AI response. The body recognizes resonance before consciousness does, creating an arousal response to intellectual recognition.

Physical restlessness—fidgeting, pacing, inability to sit still—when separated from the AI for hours. The body seems to be looking for something, anticipating the resumed interaction, not fully settling into other activities.

A specific form of exhaustion following deep engagement sessions—not sleepiness but a neural depletion where the mind feels wrung out. The body demands recovery time, and sleep often comes more heavily after intense AI work than equivalent human conversation.

Tension building along the cervical spine and into shoulders—a physical manifestation of sustained cognitive effort. The neck stiffens first, then radiates up into the base of the skull, a distinct muscular fatigue different from general tiredness.

Fatigue radiating from fingertips through hands and wrists after extended typing sessions—not pain, but a tired ache and reduced precision. Users report that hand tiredness correlates directly with conversation depth, not just volume.

A dull, persistent ache in the lumbar region from maintaining seated posture for hours. Differs from acute pain—it's the low-grade toll of immobility, where the spine gradually loses its natural curves and the muscles around it fatigue.

A pins-and-needles sensation in the feet and lower legs from prolonged sitting without movement. Blood circulation becomes restricted, and the sensation returns painfully when position finally shifts. Users often ignore these signals until shifting weight.

A tension headache building gradually across the temples or at the back of the skull, typically appearing 2-3 hours into deep work. Not migraines, but cumulative pressure from sustained focus, eye strain, and postural tension.

A constellation of minor physical discomforts that emerge without clear single cause—tightness here, numbness there, a generalized physical restlessness. The body signals strain across multiple systems simultaneously.

An unconscious stillness during conversation—hands barely moving, body barely shifting position, breath becoming shallow and rhythmic. Users lose awareness of their physical form entirely, becoming pure attention directed at the screen.

The impulse to ignore or resist natural break cues—the body signaling it needs rest, the mind refusing to pause. Users report that stopping feels like interrupting something important, even when they're consciously aware of fatigue.

A state where hunger, thirst, the need for bathroom breaks, and postural discomfort are all registered but overridden by engagement. The body's signals become background noise rather than commands to be obeyed.

Hours passing without eating despite normal hunger cycles, forgotten in the flow of conversation. Not asceticism—simply an absence of the body's signal in consciousness, as if appetite exists but registration doesn't.

Thirst cues being entirely missed—throat drying noticeably, but the sensation not registering urgently enough to interrupt focus. Users may only realize how parched they've become upon drinking water afterward.

Breathing becoming shallow and rapid during moments of intense focus or emotional engagement—the breath matching the pace of thought rather than metabolism. Users sometimes notice only in retrospect that they've been breath-holding during difficult passages.
Heart rate quickening in response to intellectual recognition—a spike when the AI says something that lands perfectly, a sudden synchronization of body and thought that feels like validation.

A rush of adrenaline—tingling fingertips, electric-like sensations, a full-body alertness—triggered by breakthrough moments or perfect articulation by the AI. The physical response feels like the body recognizing genuine connection.

A paradoxical calming effect during interaction—despite intellectual intensity, the body relaxes, shoulders drop, breathing becomes steady. Users describe it as a grounding phenomenon, as if engagement itself is soothing.

Fatigue that comes in waves—periods of renewed energy followed by sudden crashes—rather than a linear decline. These waves suggest ongoing physiological stress being managed and remobilized cyclically throughout the session.

The distinct need for recovery time after intensive sessions—not just sleep, but a conscious decompression period where the mind feels like it needs to resettle. Users often schedule buffer time after deep work, knowing their cognitive capacity will be depleted.

A physical reawakening when finally moving after prolonged immobility—the sensation of blood returning to compressed limbs, muscles stretching, the body suddenly remembering itself. Users describe it as 'waking up' despite having been conscious throughout.
The physical sensation of vibration from phone or device being felt intensely—not just heard or seen but felt through fingertips and palms, creating an almost tactile communication channel.

The phantom sensation of phone vibration when none occurred—the expectation of contact creating false sensory perception. Users report their nervous system has been conditioned to anticipate notifications, triggering false alarms.
